messages.content:
- name: Comparing last updated kernel and running kernel
    shell: |
      LAST_KERNEL=$(rpm -q --last kernel |head -1 | awk '{print $1}' | sed 's/kernel-//');
      CURRENT_KERNEL=$(uname -r)
      if [[ $LAST_KERNEL != $CURRENT_KERNEL ]]; then
        # Set reboot flag
        touch /tmp/reboot
        echo "KERNEL_CHANGED"
      else
        echo "No KERNEL Change Detected"
      fi
    register: kernel_update_status
    
  - name: Reboot Requirement
    debug:
     msg: "KERNEL_CHANGED"
    when: kernel_update_status == "KERNEL_CHANGED" 
    
  - name: Reboot the machine (Wait for 5 min or 300 Sec)
    reboot:
      reboot_timeout: 300
      test_command: uptime
    when: kernel_update_status == "KERNEL_CHANGED" 
    register: reboot_status
      
  - name : Wait for server to come back online after restart.
    wait_for_connection:
      delay : '10'
      timeout : '500'
      
  - meta : end_play

- name: Comparing last updated kernel and running kernel
    shell: |
      LAST_KERNEL=$(rpm -q --last kernel |head -1 | awk '{print $1}' | sed 's/kernel-//');
      CURRENT_KERNEL=$(uname -r)
      if [[ $LAST_KERNEL != $CURRENT_KERNEL ]]; then
        # Set reboot flag
        touch /tmp/reboot
        echo "KERNEL_CHANGED"
      else
        echo "No KERNEL Change Detected"
      fi
    register: kernel_update_status
    
  - name: Reboot Requirement
    debug:
     msg: "KERNEL_CHANGED"
    when: kernel_update_status.stdout == "KERNEL_CHANGED" 
    
  - name: Reboot the machine (Wait for 5 min or 300 Sec)
    reboot:
      reboot_timeout: 300
      test_command: uptime
    when: kernel_update_status.stdout == "KERNEL_CHANGED" 
    register: reboot_status
      
  - name : Wait for server to come back online after restart.
    wait_for_connection:
      delay : '10'
      timeout : '500'
      
  - meta : end_play
give me a command that check all de kernel installed in linux oracle 8

To check all the installed kernels on Oracle Linux 8, you can use the following command:

```
rpm -q kernel
```

This command will list all the installed kernel packages along with their version numbers.

The `uname -r` command is used in Linux systems to display the kernel release information. 

The `uname` command stands for "Unix Name", and the `-r` option means "kernel release". When you run `uname -r` in the terminal, it will display the version number of the running kernel on your system.

For example, if you run the command `uname -r` on your Oracle Linux 8 system, it will display the version number of the running kernel. The output will be something like `4.18.0-80.11.2.el8_0.x86_64`, which means that the version of the running kernel is 4.18.0-80.11.2.el8_0.x86_64.

To uninstall a kernel on Oracle Linux, you can use the `dnf remove` command followed by the name of the kernel package.

Here is an example command that can be used to remove a kernel:
```
sudo dnf remove kernel-<version>
```

Replace `<version>` with the version number of the kernel that you want to uninstall. 

For example, if you want to remove kernel version `4.18.0-80.11.2.el8_0.x86_64`, you would run the command:
```
sudo dnf remove kernel-4.18.0-80.11.2.el8_0.x86_64
```

After running this command, the kernel will be uninstalled, and you can free up some space on your system. However, it is generally a good practice to keep at least two kernel versions installed on the system at any given time in case there are issues with the latest kernel version.
how to downgrade the kernel version

To downgrade the kernel version on Oracle Linux, you will need to first install the previous version of the kernel that you want to downgrade to, and then set the system to boot from the previous kernel version.

Here are the steps to downgrade the kernel version:

1. First, check the current kernel version running on your system using the `uname -r` command.

2. Install the previous version of the kernel using the following command:
   ```
   sudo dnf install kernel-<version>
   ```
   Replace `<version>` with the version number of the kernel that you want to install.
   
3. After the kernel installation is complete, edit the `/etc/default/grub` file and set the value of `GRUB_DEFAULT` to the index of the installed previous kernel version. For example, if the index of the previous kernel version is `2`, set `GRUB_DEFAULT=2` in the file.

4. Generate a new Grub2 configuration file using the following command:
   ```
   sudo grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
   ```

5. Reboot the system to boot from the previous kernel version.

After completing these steps, your system will be running on the previous kernel version. Note that downgrading the kernel version may introduce security risks, so it should be done with caution. It is recommended that you keep your kernel up to date with the latest security patches and bug fixes.
how to automatic change to the latest kernel oracle linux 9

In Oracle Linux 9, the default boot-order will automatically boot the latest kernel when it is installed. So, in most cases, you don't need to make any changes to ensure that the system boots the latest kernel.

However, if you have multiple kernel versions installed on your system and you want to ensure that the latest kernel is always selected during boot, you can follow these steps:

1. Check the latest kernel version installed on your system using the `rpm -q kernel` command.

2. Open the `/etc/dnf/dnf.conf` file in an editor using the command:
   ```
   sudo nano /etc/dnf/dnf.conf
   ```

3. Add the following line to the file to set the default kernel version:
   ```
   installonly_limit=1
   ```
   This will remove all old kernel versions except the most recent one.

4. Save the changes to the file and exit the editor.

5. If you want to remove the older kernel versions manually, you can use the command:
   ```
   sudo dnf remove kernel-<version>
   ```
   Replace `<version>` with the version number of the kernel that you want to uninstall.

After completing these steps, your system will always boot the latest kernel version by default. However, it is generally recommended to keep at least two kernel versions installed on the system to allow rollbacks in case there are issues with the latest kernel version.

- name: Update kernel
  hosts: all
  tasks:
    - name: Install latest kernel
      dnf:
        name: kernel-core # or "kernel" for Red Hat compatible kernels
        state: latest
    - name: Reboot if necessary
      reboot:
        reboot_timeout: 300
        test_command: uptime
      when: ansible_kernel | version_compare('4.18', '<')

This Ansible playbook will update the kernel on all hosts in the inventory and reboot the system if necessary. Here's what each task does:

- `Install latest kernel` task uses the `dnf` module to install the latest kernel package available. The `kernel-core` package is used for Oracle Linux kernels, but you can use `kernel` package for Red Hat compatible kernels.
- `Reboot if necessary` task uses the `reboot` module to reboot the system if the kernel version is less than `4.18`. The `ansible_kernel` variable contains the current kernel version running on each host, and the `version_compare` method is used to compare it with `4.18`.

This playbook will ensure that all hosts are running the latest kernel and rebooted if necessary. However, before running this playbook, you should ensure that it is appropriate for your system and make sure to take appropriate backup and precautions for any critical production system.
how to remove all old kernels oracle Linux 9.0 in command line

To remove all old kernels from Oracle Linux 9.0 using command line, you can follow these steps:

1. List all the installed kernel packages using the following command:
   ```
   rpm -qa kernel\* | sort
   ```

2. Identify the package names of all the older kernel versions that you want to remove.

3. Use the `dnf` command to remove the older kernel packages using the following command:
   ```
   sudo dnf remove kernel-<version>
   ```
   Replace `<version>` with the package name of the older kernel version that you want to remove. Run this command for all the identified older kernel versions.

4. After removing the older kernel versions, regenerate the grub configuration file using the following command:
   ```
   sudo grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
   ```

5. Reboot the system to ensure that it boots successfully on the latest kernel version.

After following these steps, all the outdated kernel versions will be removed, and the system will boot with only the latest kernel version. Note that it is usually recommended to keep at least two kernel versions installed on the system to allow for rollbacks in case there are issues with the latest kernel version.

---
- name: Check kernel versions
  remote_user: root
  hosts: all
  become: true
  ignore_errors: yes
  
  tasks:
    - name: Get last installed kernel and current kernel
      ansible.builtin.shell: >
        LAST_KERNEL=$(rpm -q --last kernel |head -1 | awk '{print 1}' | sed 's/kernel-//'); 
        CURRENT_KERNEL=(uname -r);       
        echo "Last kernel: LAST_KERNEL, Current kernel: CURRENT_KERNEL"
      register: kernel_output

    - name: Display kernel versions
      ansible.builtin.debug:
        var: kernel_output.stdout

This Ansible playbook is used to check the kernel versions of all hosts in the inventory. Here's what each task does:

- The `Get last installed kernel and current kernel` task uses the `shell` module to get the last installed kernel and the current running kernel versions on each host. The `rpm -q --last kernel` command is used to get the last installed kernel version, and `uname -r` command is used to get the current kernel version. The `$()` is used to execute the command inside it first and then output the result. The `awk` and `sed` commands are used to extract the kernel version number from the `rpm` output. The `echo` command prints the kernel version results in the output.

- The `Display kernel versions` task uses the `debug` module to print the `kernel_output.stdout` variable, which contains the output of the first task.

This playbook will help you to identify the kernel versions of the hosts in your inventory. However, it doesn't perform any actions to update or remove the kernel versions. You can modify this playbook to perform such actions based on your requirements.
